title: Dog Paw Pad Burns: Treating Blisters from Hot Pavement
meta_desc: Learn how to treat dog paw blisters from hot pavement. Discover when to see a vet and why a photo is essential for triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Paw pad burns are painful thermal injuries that typically require veterinary care within 24 hours, though cases involving sloughing or inability to walk are immediate emergencies.

What exactly is a paw pad burn and how do blisters form?

  • A paw pad burn occurs when a dog walks on hot surfaces like asphalt, sand, or concrete.
  • While paw pads are tougher than standard skin, they are still susceptible to thermal injuries.
  • A blister indicates a second-degree burn where the top layer of the pad separates from the sensitive tissue underneath, often filling with fluid.

Is a paw pad blister considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level is Medium; while rarely life-threatening, these injuries are extremely painful.
  • Wounds carry a high risk of infection because dogs must walk on the injured tissue.
  • You should seek veterinary care within 24 hours for standard pad burns.
  • Visit an emergency clinic immediately if your dog cannot walk, the pad is sloughing off entirely, or there is significant bleeding.

How can a photo of my dog's paw help with veterinary triage?

  • A clear, well-lit photo allows a professional to assess the severity of the burn remotely.
  • Visuals help determine if the blister is intact or ruptured and reveal the color of the underlying tissue.
  • Photos can show early signs of secondary infection.
  • This information helps decide if your dog needs immediate intervention or can be managed with at-home first aid until an appointment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

For second-degree burns associated with blisters on dog paw pads, the vesicles should be left intact to protect against infection. An antibacterial dressing, such as silver sulfadiazine, should be applied to the wounds while an eschar is allowed to form. In uninfected burns, sterile skin protectants can be applied to act as a second skin. For severe burns, fluid therapy is indicated to offset fluid losses, and systemic antibiotics may be required to prevent secondary infection. Analgesics should be provided for pain management.

Chapter: Emergency, Dermatology, General Principles

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1720)

faqs.question: How can I tell if the pavement is too hot for my dog?
faqs.answer: Place the back of your hand on the pavement for seven seconds. If it is too hot for you to hold your hand there comfortably, it is too hot for your dog's paws.

faqs.question: Can I put human antibiotic ointment on my dog's burn?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid using human ointments unless directed by a vet, as dogs often lick their paws and may ingest ingredients that are toxic or cause stomach upset.

faqs.question: What is the immediate first aid for a paw burn?
faqs.answer: Flush the area with cool (not ice-cold) water for 10 to 15 minutes. Gently pat dry and keep the dog off their feet and away from the burned area to prevent licking.
